one_line_takeaway: SpecTemp analytically derives a dimensionality-adaptive spectral tempering exponent from the corpus eigenspectrum's SNR profile, closely matching grid-searched-oracle post-hoc compression performance across six dense retrievers without any labeled data or hyperparameter tuning — this is an information-retrieval efficiency paper, not a security paper.
key_finding: SpecTemp matches or outperforms all fixed-gamma spectral baselines (PCA, standard whitening, fixed gamma=0.5 whitening) in the majority of configurations without tuning. Representative Table 2 results: on Qwen3-8B/NQ at k=768, SpecTemp scores 64.9 nDCG@10 (not significantly different from Full Dimension 64.9) versus PCA 64.6 and gamma-Whitening 64.1; on GTE-7B/NQ at k=768, SpecTemp reaches 67.2 versus Full Dimension 66.8, PCA 67.1, and Whitening 64.4. On a grid-search comparison (Table 3, GTE-7B on NQ), the analytically predicted gamma(k) tracked the oracle gamma*(k) closely (e.g., 0.15 vs 0.15 at k=768, 0.96 vs 0.55 at k=128), with resulting nDCG@10 performance loss of at most 0.11 points (0-100 scale) and an average |delta| of 0.05 points across the five k values. A sensitivity analysis (Section 4.2.4) found nDCG@10 varies by at most 0.03 points when the noise-floor tail set size T is changed from 5% to 20%, supporting the claim that the 10% default requires no per-task tuning.
core_contribution: The paper proposes Spectral Tempering (SpecTemp), a learning-free, post-hoc embedding-compression method for dense passage retrieval. It observes that the optimal spectral-scaling exponent gamma in fractional-power dimension weighting (interpolating between PCA, gamma=0, and standard whitening, gamma=1) is not a fixed hyperparameter but should vary with the target dimensionality k, governed by the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) of the retained subspace. SpecTemp estimates a spectral noise floor from the tail 10% of the corpus covariance eigenvalues, computes a local SNR(i) per eigen-rank, locates a 'knee point' of the SNR curve via the Kneedle algorithm as an anchor, and derives an adaptive gamma(k) = min(1, SNR(k)/SNR(k_knee)) analytically, with no labeled data, retraining, or validation-based grid search required.
threat_model: Not applicable. This is an efficiency/representation-learning paper on embedding compression for dense retrieval; it defines no adversary, attacker capability, or attack surface. The only mentions of 'robustness,' 'query perturbations,' and 'corpus poisoning' occur in the Related Work section as citations to other papers by the same first author (refs [14], [15], [16], [20], [28], [43]) describing prior work on adversarial robustness of dense retrievers — this is not part of SpecTemp's own contribution or evaluation, and the paper does not test SpecTemp under any adversarial condition.
limitations: The paper's own text: (1) it evaluates only six embedding models and four datasets, all English text retrieval; (2) it explicitly excludes quantization-based methods (e.g., Product Quantization) from comparison, framing them as complementary rather than competing, so no combined evaluation is reported; (3) the SNR formalization is stated by the authors to be 'not intended as a generative statistical estimate in the sense of spiked covariance models, but as a monotonic, spectrum-level proxy'; (4) at k=128 on GTE-7B/NQ the predicted gamma diverges substantially from the oracle in parameter space (0.55 vs 0.96) even though the resulting performance gap is small, indicating the method can land in a different but similarly-performing point on a flat optimization landscape rather than truly recovering the oracle; (5) the Conclusion states future work is needed to examine interaction with index-side compression (e.g., PQ) and impact on end-to-end deployment metrics beyond retrieval effectiveness alone, meaning those questions are unaddressed by this paper.

quantitative_results: Qwen3-8B, NQ, k=768: SpecTemp 64.9 nDCG@10 (ns vs Full Dimension) vs PCA 64.6 (ns), Whitening 61.9, gamma-Whitening 64.1, Full Dimension 64.9
GTE-7B, NQ, k=768: SpecTemp 67.2 vs Full Dimension 66.8, PCA 67.1 (ns), Whitening 64.4, gamma-Whitening 66.4 (ns)

GTE-7B, NQ, oracle-vs-predicted gamma (Table 3): k=768 oracle 0.15 / predicted 0.15; k=512 oracle 0.25 / predicted 0.24; k=256 oracle 0.45 / predicted 0.49; k=128 oracle 0.55 / predicted 0.96 (|delta| nDCG@10 = 0.11); k=64 oracle 0.95 / predicted 1.00; average |delta| nDCG@10 = 0.05 points (0-100 scale)
Sensitivity to tail set T size (5%-20%) on GTE-7B->NQ: nDCG@10 varies by at most 0.03 points
Jina-v4, MS MARCO, k=768: SpecTemp 31.9 (ns vs Full Dimension 32.1) vs PCA 31.9 (ns), Whitening 29.0, gamma-Whitening 31.3
